title: Requests, Limits, QoS, Probes, Startup, Readiness, and Resource Failure · Cluster Case: K8s Startup Readiness Liveness
summary: A slow-starting API is killed by liveness checks, serves traffic before warmup, and competes for memory without meaningful requests or limits. Planned artifact: Add and verify the k8s startup readiness liveness increment in health and resource behavior policy. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Separate startup gating, traffic readiness, process liveness, container restart, endpoint conditions, and dependency health.
Derive Guaranteed, Burstable, and BestEffort QoS, container OOM, Pod eviction, node pressure, and restart evidence from effective resources.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# kubernetes-basics cumulative orchestration portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never invoke kubectl, read kubeconfig, contact a cluster API, or execute host commands.
workspace: kubernetes
version-baseline:
  kubernetes: 1.36.2
  minikube: 1.38.1
  gateway-api: 1.5.1
  helm: 4.2.2
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-cluster
evidence:

steps.title: Predict K8s Startup Readiness Liveness · Theory K8s Startup Readiness Liveness
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory service passes liveness while serving stale data, has no startup probe, requests 100m but uses 1000m CPU, and is OOMKilled under pressure. Redesign probes, requests, limits, QoS, graceful termination, and changed-load checks. Competency probe: Separate startup gating, traffic readiness, process liveness, container restart, endpoint conditions, and dependency health.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Name context, namespace, API identity, desired spec, observed status, controller authority, workload, network, storage, identity, policy, and failure-domain boundaries; predict reconciliation; review the smallest deterministic manifest change; then verify a failure, repair, rollback, and disposable-cluster transfer limit.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: A worked example service passes liveness while serving stale data, has no startup probe, requests 100m but uses 1000m CPU, and is OOMKilled under pressure. Redesign probes, requests, limits, QoS, graceful termination, and changed-load checks. Competency probe: Separate startup gating, traffic readiness, process liveness, container restart, endpoint conditions, and dependency health. A defensible solution applies K8s Startup Readiness Liveness, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to health and resource behavior policy.

content.code:
# Evidence: k8s-worked-k8s-health-resources-qos-5
  - competency: k8s-worked-k8s-health-resources-qos-5
    artifact: health-resource-policy.yaml
    decision: worked k8s health resources qos 5
    environment: managed canary cluster with provider-specific load balancing
    fault-injection: drop one metrics series during an autoscaling surge
    retained-proof: accessible incident timeline with causal regression evidence
    case-key: worked5
    change: |
      requests: {cpu: 200m, memory: 256Mi}
      limits: {memory: 512Mi}
      startupProbe: bounded-cold-start
      readinessProbe: traffic-contract
      livenessProbe: process-deadlock-only
      fault-test: throttling-and-oom
    verify: kubectl get pod status-api -o json && kubectl top pod status-api --containers
    failure: Reject dependency-based liveness, requests as runtime caps, green idle probes as capacity proof, or unexplained OOM and eviction.
    repair: Measure workload demand, separate probe purposes, calculate QoS, inject startup and pressure faults, and correlate container and node evidence.
content.caption: Runnable Kubernetes object and transfer-evidence contract example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.title: Non-worked case
content.text: Liveness should fail whenever any external dependency is temporarily unavailable. This fails the K8s Startup Readiness Liveness contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.
